title: Architecture Patterns
description: MVVM, MVI, Repository pattern, UiState, UseCases, and architecture at scale

references.title: Android Developers — Guide to App Architecture

lesson:
Android Architecture Guide

Google recommends MVVM with Unidirectional Data Flow: state flows down (ViewModel → UI), events flow up (UI → ViewModel). This makes your app predictable, testable, and maintainable.

The Architecture Layers

  • UI Layer — Composables observe ViewModel state
  • ViewModel — Holds UI state, handles business logic, survives config changes
  • Domain Layer (optional) — UseCase/Interactor classes for reusable business logic
  • Repository — Single source of truth, abstracts data sources
  • Data Sources — Network (Retrofit), local (Room), preferences (DataStore)
UI Layer Domain Layer (optional) Data Layer ┌──────────┐ ┌───────────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │Composable│────▶│ UseCase │────▶│ Repository │ │ │◀────│ (operator invoke) │◀────│ │ └──────────┘ └───────────────────┘ └──────┬───────┘ │ │ ▼ ┌─────┴─────┐ ┌──────────┐ │ │ │ViewModel │ DataSource DataSource │(StateFlow│ (Network) (Local DB) │ <UiState>│ └──────────┘ State flows DOWN ↓ Events flow UP ↑

UiState Pattern

Represent screen state as a sealed class: Loading, Success(data), Error(message). The ViewModel exposes a single StateFlow<UiState>, and the UI handles each case with when.

MVI (Model-View-Intent)

MVI extends UDF by formalizing user actions as Intents (not Android Intents!) and processing them through a Reducer that produces new state. This makes state transitions explicit and debuggable.

// 1. Define user actions as sealed class sealed class HomeIntent { object LoadData : HomeIntent() data class Search(val query: String) : HomeIntent() data class ToggleFavorite(val itemId: String) : HomeIntent() } // 2. Reducer produces new state from current state + intent fun reduce(state: HomeState, intent: HomeIntent): HomeState = when (intent) { is HomeIntent.LoadData -> state.copy(isLoading = true) is HomeIntent.Search -> state.copy(query = intent.query) is HomeIntent.ToggleFavorite -> state.copy( items = state.items.map { if (it.id == intent.itemId) it.copy(isFav = !it.isFav) else it } ) }
MVVM vs MVI: MVVM exposes multiple methods on ViewModel (search(), refresh()). MVI funnels all actions through a single onIntent() entry point. MVI is better for complex screens with many interactions; MVVM is simpler for straightforward screens.

UseCase / Interactor Pattern

The Domain Layer sits between ViewModel and Repository. Each UseCase encapsulates one piece of reusable business logic. Google's convention: a single public operator fun invoke() method.

// Convention: verb + noun + UseCase class GetFilteredArticlesUseCase( private val articlesRepository: ArticlesRepository, private val userPrefsRepository: UserPrefsRepository ) { operator fun invoke(): Flow<List<Article>> { return combine( articlesRepository.getArticles(), userPrefsRepository.getPrefs() ) { articles, prefs -> articles.filter { it.topic in prefs.followedTopics } } } } // ViewModel calls it like a function: val articles = getFilteredArticlesUseCase()
When NOT to use UseCases: If a UseCase simply delegates to a single repository method without adding logic, it's unnecessary boilerplate. Only create UseCases when they combine data from multiple repositories or contain business logic shared across ViewModels.

Architecture at Scale

Architecture patterns change as apps grow. Key considerations:

  • Small app (1-3 devs): MVVM + Repository is sufficient. Domain layer is optional overhead.
  • Medium app (5-15 devs): Add UseCases for shared logic. Modularize into feature modules.
  • Large app (20+ devs): Full Clean Architecture with strict layer boundaries. Feature modules with independent navigation. Shared core modules. Convention plugins for build config.
Key Principle — SSOT (Single Source of Truth): Every data type should have exactly one owner responsible for mutations. For user data, it's UserRepository. For UI state, it's the ViewModel. Multiple sources of truth = inconsistent state = bugs.

The Complete Data Flow — From API to UI

Every Android feature follows the same pipeline. Data starts at the network or database, passes through a Repository, gets transformed, and lands in the ViewModel's StateFlow for the UI to observe. Understanding this pipeline end-to-end is the key to building real features.

API (JSON) Repository (suspend) ViewModel (StateFlow) UI (Compose) ───────────── ─────────────────────── ────────────────────── ─────────────────── Retrofit dto.toDomain() _uiState.value = ... collectAsState() catch / map viewModelScope.launch when (state) { } Flow or suspend sealed UiState recomposition

The following five patterns cover the most common real-world scenarios. Each builds on the previous one.

Pattern 1: Single API Call → Transform → UI

The simplest pattern: fetch data from a single API endpoint, map the DTO to a domain model, and expose it as a sealed UiState.

// ── Data Layer ── // Network DTO (what the API returns) data class UserDto( val id: String, val full_name: String, val avatar_url: String? ) // Domain model (what the app uses) data class User( val id: String, val displayName: String, val avatarUrl: String ) // Mapper: DTO → Domain fun UserDto.toDomain() = User( id = id, displayName = full_name, avatarUrl = avatar_url ?: "https://example.com/default.png" ) // Repository with suspend function class UserRepository( private val api: UserApi ) { suspend fun getUser(userId: String): User { return api.fetchUser(userId).toDomain() } }
// ── ViewModel Layer ── sealed class UserUiState { data object Loading : UserUiState() data class Success(val user: User) : UserUiState() data class Error(val message: String) : UserUiState() } class UserViewModel( private val repository: UserRepository, private val savedStateHandle: SavedStateHandle ) : ViewModel() { private val _uiState = MutableStateFlow<UserUiState>(UserUiState.Loading) val uiState: StateFlow<UserUiState> = _uiState.asStateFlow() // ── Three ways to trigger loading ── // (a) Init block — loads immediately when ViewModel is created init { loadUser("user-123") } // (b) Called from LaunchedEffect(userId) in Compose // (c) SavedStateHandle automatic — see below fun loadUser(userId: String) { viewModelScope.launch { _uiState.value = UserUiState.Loading try { val user = repository.getUser(userId) _uiState.value = UserUiState.Success(user) } catch (e: Exception) { _uiState.value = UserUiState.Error( e.message ?: "Unknown error" ) } } } }
// ── SavedStateHandle Pattern (c) — No loadUser() needed ── class UserViewModel( private val repository: UserRepository, savedStateHandle: SavedStateHandle ) : ViewModel() { // userId comes from navigation arguments automatically private val userId: String = savedStateHandle["userId"]!! val uiState: StateFlow<UserUiState> = flow { emit(UserUiState.Loading) try { val user = repository.getUser(userId) emit(UserUiState.Success(user)) } catch (e: Exception) { emit(UserUiState.Error(e.message ?: "Unknown error")) } }.stateIn( scope = viewModelScope, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5_000), initialValue = UserUiState.Loading ) }
// ── UI Layer (Compose) ── @Composable fun UserScreen( viewModel: UserViewModel = hiltViewModel() ) { val uiState by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() when (val state = uiState) { is UserUiState.Loading -> CircularProgressIndicator() is UserUiState.Success -> UserCard(user = state.user) is UserUiState.Error -> ErrorWithRetry( message = state.message, onRetry = { viewModel.loadUser("user-123") } ) } } // LaunchedEffect trigger (way b) — reloads when userId changes @Composable fun UserScreen( userId: String, viewModel: UserViewModel = hiltViewModel() ) { LaunchedEffect(userId) { viewModel.loadUser(userId) } val uiState by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() // ... same when block ... } // Pull-to-refresh @Composable fun UserScreenWithRefresh( viewModel: UserViewModel = hiltViewModel() ) { val uiState by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() val isRefreshing = uiState is UserUiState.Loading PullToRefreshBox( isRefreshing = isRefreshing, onRefresh = { viewModel.loadUser("user-123") } ) { when (val state = uiState) { is UserUiState.Success -> UserCard(user = state.user) is UserUiState.Error -> ErrorMessage(state.message) is UserUiState.Loading -> { /* handled by indicator */ } } } }
Three loading triggers compared:
(a) init { } — simple, loads once, no dynamic arguments
(b) LaunchedEffect(key) — reloads when key changes, good for navigation args
(c) SavedStateHandle — automatic, type-safe, survives process death, recommended by Google

Pattern 2: Chained API Calls (Sequential Dependency)

Often one API call depends on data from another. The key insight: use sequential calls when there is a dependency, and parallel calls (with async) when there is not.

// ── Sequential: second call depends on first ── class OrderRepository( private val api: OrderApi ) { suspend fun getOrderWithItems(orderId: String): OrderWithItems { // Step 1: get the order (contains itemIds) val order = api.fetchOrder(orderId) // Step 2: fetch items using IDs from Step 1 val items = api.fetchItems(order.itemIds) return OrderWithItems( order = order.toDomain(), items = items.map { it.toDomain() } ) } } // ── Parallel: independent calls with async ── class DashboardRepository( private val api: DashboardApi ) { suspend fun getDashboard(userId: String): Dashboard { return coroutineScope { // Both calls start simultaneously val profileDeferred = async { api.fetchProfile(userId) } val statsDeferred = async { api.fetchStats(userId) } // Wait for both to finish val profile = profileDeferred.await() val stats = statsDeferred.await() Dashboard( profile = profile.toDomain(), stats = stats.toDomain() ) } } }
Sequential (dependent calls): Parallel (independent calls): fetchOrder() fetchItems() fetchProfile() fetchStats() ├────200ms────┤ ├────200ms─────┤ ├────300ms────┤ ├────300ms─────┤ Total: 500ms (sum) Total: 300ms (max) Use when: call B needs Use when: calls are data from call A independent of each other
// ── ViewModel: combining both patterns ── class OrderViewModel( private val orderRepo: OrderRepository ) : ViewModel() { private val _uiState = MutableStateFlow<OrderUiState>(OrderUiState.Loading) val uiState: StateFlow<OrderUiState> = _uiState.asStateFlow() fun loadOrder(orderId: String) { viewModelScope.launch { _uiState.value = OrderUiState.Loading try { // Repository handles sequential/parallel internally val orderWithItems = orderRepo.getOrderWithItems(orderId) _uiState.value = OrderUiState.Success(orderWithItems) } catch (e: Exception) { _uiState.value = OrderUiState.Error(e.message ?: "Failed") } } } }
Never use GlobalScope for parallel calls. Use coroutineScope { } inside a suspend function. It guarantees structured concurrency: if one call fails, the other is automatically cancelled. GlobalScope leaks coroutines and ignores cancellation.

Pattern 3: Reactive Database → Transform → UI (Room Flow)

Instead of a one-shot suspend function, Room can return a Flow that emits whenever the database changes. The ViewModel uses stateIn() to convert this Flow into a StateFlow — no launch needed.

// ── Room DAO returns Flow (reactive) ── @Dao interface NoteDao { @Query("SELECT * FROM notes ORDER BY updated_at DESC") fun observeAll(): Flow<List<NoteEntity>> // Compare: one-shot suspend (Pattern 1 style) @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE id = :id") suspend fun getById(id: String): NoteEntity? } // ── Repository maps entity → domain ── class NoteRepository( private val dao: NoteDao ) { fun observeNotes(): Flow<List<Note>> { return dao.observeAll().map { entities -> entities.map { it.toDomain() } } } }
// ── ViewModel: stateIn() converts Flow → StateFlow ── class NoteListViewModel( private val repository: NoteRepository ) : ViewModel() { // No launch needed! stateIn() handles collection. val uiState: StateFlow<NoteListUiState> = repository .observeNotes() .map { notes -> NoteListUiState.Success(notes) } .catch { e -> emit(NoteListUiState.Error(e.message ?: "Error")) } .stateIn( scope = viewModelScope, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5_000), initialValue = NoteListUiState.Loading ) }
Pattern 1 (one-shot): Pattern 3 (reactive Flow): ViewModel calls loadUser() Room emits automatically │ │ ▼ ▼ repo.getUser() ←── suspend dao.observeAll() ←── Flow │ │ ▼ ▼ _uiState.value = result .stateIn(viewModelScope) │ │ ▼ ▼ UI recomposes ONCE UI recomposes on EVERY DB change Use when: fetch once Use when: data changes over time (user profile, API call) (notes, messages, settings)
SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5_000) keeps the Flow active for 5 seconds after the last collector disappears (e.g., during a config change). This avoids restarting the database query on rotation. Eagerly starts immediately and never stops — wastes resources if no one is collecting.

Pattern 4: Write + Read (Optimistic UI)

When the user creates or edits data, write to Room immediately so the UI updates instantly from the reactive Flow. Then sync to the server in the background with WorkManager. If the sync fails, show a pending indicator.

// ── Repository: write locally, schedule background sync ── class NoteRepository( private val dao: NoteDao, private val syncScheduler: SyncScheduler ) { fun observeNotes(): Flow<List<Note>> = dao.observeAll().map { entities -> entities.map { it.toDomain() } } suspend fun saveNote(note: Note) { // 1. Write to Room immediately → Flow emits → UI updates dao.upsert(note.toEntity().copy(syncStatus = SyncStatus.PENDING)) // 2. Schedule background sync via WorkManager syncScheduler.scheduleNoteSync(note.id) } } // ── Sync status enum persisted in Room ── enum class SyncStatus { SYNCED, PENDING, FAILED } // ── SyncScheduler wraps WorkManager ── class SyncScheduler(private val workManager: WorkManager) { fun scheduleNoteSync(noteId: String) { val request = OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<NoteSyncWorker>() .setInputData(workDataOf("noteId" to noteId)) .setConstraints( Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.CONNECTED) .build() ) .setBackoffCriteria( BackoffPolicy.EXPONENTIAL, Duration.ofSeconds(30) ) .build() workManager.enqueueUniqueWork( "sync-note-$noteId", ExistingWorkPolicy.REPLACE, request ) } }
// ── WorkManager Worker: syncs to server ── class NoteSyncWorker( context: Context, params: WorkerParameters, private val api: NoteApi, private val dao: NoteDao ) : CoroutineWorker(context, params) { override suspend fun doWork(): Result { val noteId = inputData.getString("noteId") ?: return Result.failure() val note = dao.getById(noteId) ?: return Result.failure() return try { api.uploadNote(note.toDto()) dao.updateSyncStatus(noteId, SyncStatus.SYNCED) Result.success() } catch (e: Exception) { if (runAttemptCount < 3) { Result.retry() // exponential backoff } else { dao.updateSyncStatus(noteId, SyncStatus.FAILED) Result.failure() } } } } // ── ViewModel: just collects the reactive Flow ── class NoteListViewModel( private val repository: NoteRepository ) : ViewModel() { val uiState: StateFlow<NoteListUiState> = repository .observeNotes() .map { notes -> NoteListUiState.Success(notes) } .stateIn( scope = viewModelScope, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5_000), initialValue = NoteListUiState.Loading ) fun createNote(title: String, body: String) { viewModelScope.launch { repository.saveNote( Note(id = UUID.randomUUID().toString(), title = title, body = body, syncStatus = SyncStatus.PENDING) ) // No need to manually update UI — // Room Flow emits automatically! } } } // ── Compose UI shows sync status ── @Composable fun NoteItem(note: Note) { Row { Text(note.title) Spacer(Modifier.weight(1f)) when (note.syncStatus) { SyncStatus.SYNCED -> Icon(Icons.Default.Cloud, "Synced") SyncStatus.PENDING -> Icon(Icons.Default.Sync, "Syncing...") SyncStatus.FAILED -> Icon(Icons.Default.Warning, "Sync failed") } } }
Optimistic UI requires conflict resolution. If two devices edit the same note while offline, you need a strategy: last-write-wins, server-wins, or manual merge. For most apps, server-wins with a \"your changes were overwritten\" toast is the simplest approach.

Pattern 5: Search-as-you-type (debounce + flatMapLatest)

For search, the user types continuously. You need to: (1) wait for them to pause (debounce), (2) cancel the previous search when new input arrives (flatMapLatest), and (3) expose results as a StateFlow.

// ── Repository ── class SearchRepository( private val api: SearchApi ) { suspend fun search(query: String): List<SearchResult> { return api.search(query).map { it.toDomain() } } } // ── ViewModel with debounce + flatMapLatest ── class SearchViewModel( private val repository: SearchRepository ) : ViewModel() { // The query text — updated by the TextField private val _query = MutableStateFlow("") val searchResults: StateFlow<SearchUiState> = _query .debounce(300) // Wait 300ms after last keystroke .filter { it.length >= 2 } // Don't search for "a" .flatMapLatest { query -> flow { emit(SearchUiState.Loading) try { val results = repository.search(query) emit(SearchUiState.Success(results)) } catch (e: Exception) { emit(SearchUiState.Error(e.message ?: "Search failed")) } } } .stateIn( scope = viewModelScope, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5_000), initialValue = SearchUiState.Empty ) fun onQueryChanged(newQuery: String) { _query.value = newQuery } } sealed class SearchUiState { data object Empty : SearchUiState() data object Loading : SearchUiState() data class Success(val results: List<SearchResult>) : SearchUiState() data class Error(val message: String) : SearchUiState() }
// ── Compose UI: TextField connected to ViewModel ── @Composable fun SearchScreen( viewModel: SearchViewModel = hiltViewModel() ) { val uiState by viewModel.searchResults.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() Column { // TextField drives the query MutableStateFlow var text by remember { mutableStateOf("") } OutlinedTextField( value = text, onValueChange = { text = it viewModel.onQueryChanged(it) }, label = { Text("Search") }, modifier = Modifier.fillMaxWidth() ) Spacer(Modifier.height(16.dp)) when (val state = uiState) { is SearchUiState.Empty -> Text("Type to search...") is SearchUiState.Loading -> CircularProgressIndicator() is SearchUiState.Success -> LazyColumn { items(state.results) { result -> SearchResultItem(result) } } is SearchUiState.Error -> Text("Error: ${state.message}", color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.error) } } }
flatMapLatest prevents race conditions: User types: "k" → "ko" → "kot" → "kotl" → "kotli" → "kotlin" Without flatMapLatest (flatMap): With flatMapLatest: ┌──search("k")─────────result──┐ ┌──search("k")──CANCELLED ├──search("ko")───────result───┤ ├──search("ko")─CANCELLED ├──search("kot")──────result───┤ ├──search("kot")CANCELLED ├──search("kotl")─────result───┤ ├──search("kotl")CANCELLED ├──search("kotli")────result───┤ ├──search("kotli")CANCELLED └──search("kotlin")───result───┘ └──search("kotlin")──result ✓ 6 results arrive in unknown order! Only the latest result arrives. UI flickers between results. Clean, predictable UI.
Why flatMapLatest and not flatMap? flatMap (or flatMapMerge) collects all inner Flows concurrently. If \"ko\" finishes after \"kotlin\", the UI shows stale results. flatMapLatest cancels the previous inner Flow when a new value arrives, ensuring only the most recent search result is shown.

Pattern 6: Retry with Exponential Backoff

Network calls fail. A production app needs retry logic that: backs off exponentially, is cancellable, reports progress, and is testable.

// Reusable retry utility suspend fun <T> retryWithBackoff( maxAttempts: Int = 3, initialDelayMs: Long = 1000, maxDelayMs: Long = 16000, factor: Double = 2.0, onAttempt: (attempt: Int, maxAttempts: Int) -> Unit = { _, _ -> }, block: suspend () -> T ): T { var currentDelay = initialDelayMs repeat(maxAttempts - 1) { attempt -> onAttempt(attempt + 1, maxAttempts) try { return block() } catch (e: CancellationException) { throw e // NEVER catch CancellationException } catch (e: Exception) { if (attempt == maxAttempts - 2) throw e } delay(currentDelay) // Cancellable suspension point currentDelay = (currentDelay * factor).toLong().coerceAtMost(maxDelayMs) } onAttempt(maxAttempts, maxAttempts) return block() }
Why delay() matters for cancellation: delay() is a suspension point. If the coroutine is cancelled (user navigates away), delay() throws CancellationException immediately — no wasted waiting. This is why coroutine-based retry is superior to Thread.sleep() retry.

Use it in a ViewModel with progress reporting:

// ViewModel tracks retry progress via StateFlow private var currentJob: Job? = null fun sendMessage(prompt: String) { currentJob?.cancel() // Cancel previous request currentJob = viewModelScope.launch { _uiState.value = ChatState.Loading(attempt = 1, maxAttempts = 3) try { val response = retryWithBackoff( maxAttempts = 3, onAttempt = { attempt, max -> _uiState.value = ChatState.Loading(attempt, max) } ) { api.generateContent(prompt) } _uiState.value = ChatState.Success(response.text) } catch (e: CancellationException) { throw e } catch (e: Exception) { _uiState.value = ChatState.Error(e.message ?: "Failed", canRetry = true) } } } // Test with virtual time (delay skipped instantly) @Test fun \`retry succeeds on third attempt\`() = runTest { var calls = 0 val api = FakeApi { if (++calls < 3) throw IOException() else Response("OK") } val vm = ChatViewModel(api) vm.sendMessage("Hi") advanceUntilIdle() assertEquals(ChatState.Success("OK"), vm.uiState.value) assertEquals(3, calls) }

questions.title: Recommended UI Architecture
questions.description: Which architecture pattern does Google officially recommend for Android UI development?
options: MVC (Model-View-Controller)
MVP (Model-View-Presenter)
MVVM with Unidirectional Data Flow
Flux/Redux
questions.explanation: Google recommends MVVM with Unidirectional Data Flow (UDF). State flows down (ViewModel → UI), events flow up (UI → ViewModel). This is the natural fit for Jetpack Compose.
hints: Check the official Android architecture guide

questions.title: ViewModel Context Leak
questions.description: This ViewModel crashes on configuration change. Find and fix the memory leak.

checks.message: Use Application context instead of Activity context
Use AndroidViewModel or inject Application context

questions.explanation: ViewModels survive configuration changes, but Activities are destroyed and recreated. Holding an Activity reference causes a memory leak. Use AndroidViewModel with Application context instead.
hints: ViewModels outlive Activities across configuration changes
What type of Context lives as long as the app process?

questions.title: UiState Sealed Class
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to create a sealed class representing three data-loading states.

blanks.hint: Keyword before object for proper equals/hashCode

questions.explanation: A sealed class restricts subclasses to the same file, enabling exhaustive when. The three-state pattern (Loading, Success, Error) is standard for async data in Android.
hints: This keyword ensures all subclasses are known at compile time

questions.title: Repository Pattern
questions.description: Explain how this repository decides where to get data from.
options: Always fetches from network first, falling back to cache on error
Emits cached data immediately, then fetches fresh data — offline-first strategy
Only uses cache, never makes network requests
Fetches from network and cache in parallel
questions.explanation: This implements offline-first / cache-then-network: 1) Emit cached data for fast UI. 2) Fetch fresh data from API. 3) Save to Room and emit again. 4) If network fails and cache exists, silently handle it.
hints: Follow the order of operations: what gets emitted first?

questions.title: Dependency Injection
questions.description: Why is dependency injection (DI) important in Android development?
options: It makes apps run faster by pre-loading all classes
It decouples components, making code testable and swappable (e.g., fake repository in tests)
It automatically handles memory management
It's required by the Android framework
questions.explanation: DI means a class receives its dependencies from outside rather than creating them internally. This makes classes testable (inject fakes in tests), swappable (different implementations for debug/release), and loosely coupled. Hilt is Android's recommended DI framework, built on Dagger.
hints: How do you test a ViewModel that creates its own Repository internally?
Think about swapping real implementations for test fakes

questions.title: Collecting StateFlow in Compose
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to safely collect a ViewModel's StateFlow in a Composable.
questions.explanation: collectAsState() converts a StateFlow into Compose State, triggering recomposition on changes. collectAsStateWithLifecycle() is even better — it stops collecting when the UI is not visible, saving resources. when on a sealed class handles all cases exhaustively.
hints: There is a Compose extension to convert Flows to State
Sealed classes enable exhaustive branching without else

questions.title: Flow Collection Without Lifecycle
questions.description: This code wastes resources by continuing to collect the flow even when the app is in the background. Fix it.
questions.explanation: collectAsState() collects continuously, even when the app is backgrounded. For resource-intensive flows (location, sensor data), use collectAsStateWithLifecycle() which automatically stops collection when the lifecycle drops below STARTED. This saves battery and CPU. Requires androidx.lifecycle:lifecycle-runtime-compose.
hints: What happens to GPS collection when the user switches to another app?
There is a lifecycle-aware version of collectAsState

questions.title: Hilt Module
questions.description: Write a Hilt module that provides a Retrofit instance and a UserRepository. The Retrofit base URL should be "https://api.example.com/" and the repository needs both the API service and a DAO.
questions.explanation: Hilt modules tell the DI framework how to create instances. @Module marks the class, @InstallIn(SingletonComponent) scopes it to the app. @Provides functions return instances. @Singleton ensures one instance app-wide. Hilt automatically passes dependencies between providers.
hints: Each @Provides function returns one dependency
Hilt automatically resolves the parameter chain: Retrofit → UserApi → UserRepository

questions.title: MVI Reducer Pattern
questions.description: Trace what happens when a user dispatches ProfileIntent.ToggleNotifications. What is the resulting state?
options: ProfileState(name = "Alice", notificationsEnabled = true, isLoading = false)
ProfileState(name = "Alice", notificationsEnabled = false, isLoading = false)
ProfileState(name = "", notificationsEnabled = false, isLoading = false)
ProfileState(name = "Alice", notificationsEnabled = false, isLoading = true)
questions.explanation: The reducer uses state.copy(notificationsEnabled = !state.notificationsEnabled). Since notificationsEnabled was true, it flips to false. All other fields stay the same thanks to copy(). This is pure MVI: the reducer is a pure function that takes current state + intent and returns new state.
hints: copy() keeps unchanged fields and only modifies the ones you specify
The ! operator flips a boolean

questions.title: UseCase with operator invoke
questions.description: Write a GetTopArticlesUseCase that combines data from an ArticlesRepository and UserRepository. It should return the top 10 articles sorted by date, filtered to only include articles from authors the user follows.

checks.message: UseCase should have operator fun invoke()
Should combine data from both repositories
Should filter and sort the articles

questions.explanation: A UseCase encapsulates reusable business logic. The operator fun invoke() convention lets callers use it like a function: getTopArticlesUseCase(). This UseCase combines data from two repositories — exactly when the domain layer adds value. Naming convention: verb + noun + UseCase.
hints: Use combine() to merge two Flows
operator fun invoke() lets you call the class like a function

questions.title: When to Add a Domain Layer
questions.description: In Google's architecture guide, when should you add the optional Domain layer (UseCases)?
options: Always — every ViewModel should go through a UseCase
When business logic is shared between multiple ViewModels, or when you want to simplify complex ViewModels
Only in Clean Architecture — MVVM doesn't use UseCases
When you need to cache data between the ViewModel and Repository
questions.explanation: The domain layer is optional. Add UseCases when: (1) Business logic is shared across multiple ViewModels, (2) A ViewModel is becoming too complex and you want to extract logic. Don't add UseCases that simply delegate to a single repository method — that's unnecessary boilerplate.
hints: Think about when a UseCase adds value vs when it's just a pass-through

questions.title: MVI Intent-Reducer in ViewModel
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to complete an MVI-style ViewModel with a reducer.

blanks.hint: Pure function that produces new state

questions.explanation: MVI funnels all user actions through a single entry point (onIntent), which calls a pure reduce function. The reducer uses when on the sealed intent class to produce new state. _state.update { } ensures thread-safe state mutations. This pattern makes state transitions explicit and testable.
hints: The entry point receives an Intent and delegates to the reducer
The reducer is a pure function: (state, intent) → newState

questions.title: Exposed MutableStateFlow
questions.description: This ViewModel violates the UDF principle by exposing mutable state to the UI. Fix it so only immutable state is visible.
questions.explanation: In UDF, the ViewModel is the single source of truth for UI state. Exposing MutableStateFlow lets the UI mutate state directly, breaking unidirectional flow. Use the backing property pattern: private val _state = MutableStateFlow(...) + val state = _state.asStateFlow(). This ensures only the ViewModel can write state.
hints: The naming convention is _privateFlow (mutable) + publicFlow (immutable)
asStateFlow() wraps MutableStateFlow as read-only StateFlow

questions.title: flatMap vs flatMapLatest for Search
questions.description: A search-as-you-type feature uses flatMap (flatMapMerge) instead of flatMapLatest. The user types "kotlin" quickly. What problem will occur?
options: The app will crash with a ConcurrentModificationException
Search results from earlier partial queries ("k", "ko", "kot") may arrive after "kotlin" results, causing the UI to flicker and show stale results
Only the first query ("k") will be sent; the rest will be dropped
The app will work correctly but use slightly more memory
questions.explanation: flatMapMerge collects all inner Flows concurrently. If the search for "ko" takes 500ms and "kotlin" takes 200ms, the "kotlin" results appear first, then get overwritten by "ko" results — a classic race condition. flatMapLatest cancels previous inner Flows when a new value arrives, guaranteeing only the latest result is shown.
hints: What happens if an earlier, shorter query takes longer to return than a later, longer query?
Think about the order in which network responses arrive vs the order requests were made

questions.title: SavedStateHandle Automatic Loading
questions.description: Why does this ViewModel NOT have a loadUser() function, yet the user data still appears in the UI?
options: The Compose runtime calls loadUser() automatically via reflection
The flow builder runs when stateIn() starts collecting, triggered by the first UI subscriber — so loading happens automatically without an explicit function call
SavedStateHandle includes a built-in data fetcher that calls the repository
The ViewModel init block is running implicitly and calling the API
questions.explanation: The flow { } builder defines a cold Flow — it only runs when collected. stateIn() starts collecting this Flow when the first subscriber (the Composable calling collectAsStateWithLifecycle()) appears. This means loading triggers automatically the moment the UI observes the StateFlow. No explicit loadUser() is needed because savedStateHandle already provides the userId at construction time, and stateIn() handles the lifecycle.
hints: Think about when a cold Flow starts executing
What does stateIn() do when SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed is used?

questions.title: WhileSubscribed vs Eagerly
questions.description: What is the main benefit of using SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5_000) over SharingStarted.Eagerly with stateIn()?
options: WhileSubscribed is faster because it pre-loads the data
WhileSubscribed stops the upstream Flow when no one is collecting (e.g., app in background), saving resources, while the 5-second delay avoids restarting during quick configuration changes
WhileSubscribed automatically retries failed network requests
Eagerly and WhileSubscribed behave identically; it's just a naming preference
questions.explanation: Eagerly starts the upstream Flow immediately and never stops, even when the app is in the background — wasting CPU, battery, and network for database queries or location updates nobody is observing. WhileSubscribed(5_000) stops the upstream 5 seconds after the last collector disappears. The 5-second window handles configuration changes (screen rotation destroys and recreates the collector) without restarting the Flow. This is Google's recommended default.
hints: Think about what happens to a database Flow when the user switches to another app
Why 5000ms specifically? What takes a few seconds during normal app usage?

questions.title: Optimistic UI — Offline Editing
questions.description: In the optimistic UI pattern, the user creates a note while the device has no internet connection. What happens?
options: The note is lost because the API call fails immediately

The note is saved to Room and appears in the UI with a PENDING indicator; WorkManager waits for network connectivity, then syncs — if it still fails after 3 retries, the status changes to FAILED but the note is never lost
The app crashes with a NetworkOnMainThreadException
The note is queued in memory and written to Room only after the sync succeeds
questions.explanation: This is the power of optimistic UI: the note is saved to Room immediately, so the user sees it in the UI right away via the reactive Flow (Pattern 3). WorkManager's NetworkType.CONNECTED constraint means the sync work won't even attempt to run until the device has internet. When connectivity returns, WorkManager automatically triggers the worker. If it fails, exponential backoff retries up to 3 times. After all retries fail, the status changes to FAILED and the UI shows a warning icon — but the note data is safely persisted in Room and is never lost.
hints: Room is a local database — does it need internet to write?
What does WorkManager's NetworkType.CONNECTED constraint do when there's no network?
